🌡️ Temperature Forecasting using Prophet¶

📈 Forecasting Daily Minimum Temperature¶

Module 5 — Time Series Forecasting¶

Prepared by: Lovisha Jaat Algorithm: Facebook Prophet
Dataset: Daily Minimum Temperature Dataset

🎯 1. Objective¶

The objective of this notebook is to develop a time-series forecasting model using the Prophet algorithm.

The model will analyse historical daily minimum temperature observations and forecast future temperatures.

The notebook covers:

  • Data loading and inspection
  • Data preprocessing
  • Time-series visualization
  • Chronological train-test splitting
  • Prophet model building
  • Model training
  • Temperature forecasting
  • Forecast visualization
  • MAE, RMSE and MAPE evaluation
  • Future temperature forecasting

📝 2. Problem Statement¶

Weather forecasting is important for agriculture, transportation, tourism, energy management and daily planning.

The goal of this project is to build a Prophet forecasting model capable of predicting future daily minimum temperatures from historical temperature observations.

Prophet is suitable for this task because it can automatically model:

  • Long-term trends
  • Weekly seasonality
  • Yearly seasonality
  • Changes in time-series patterns
  • Missing or irregular observations

📂 3. Dataset Description¶

We will use the Daily Minimum Temperature Dataset.

The dataset contains daily minimum temperatures recorded in Melbourne, Australia.

Dataset columns¶

Column Description
Date Date of the temperature observation
Temp Daily minimum temperature in degrees Celsius

Dataset properties¶

  • Time frequency: Daily
  • Target variable: Minimum temperature
  • Problem type: Univariate time-series forecasting
  • Measurement unit: Degrees Celsius

⚙️ 4. Installing Prophet¶

Prophet is an external Python library and may not be installed by default.

Run the following cell once. If Prophet is already installed, you can skip this step.

In [ ]:
%pip install prophet -q

📦 5. Import Required Libraries¶

The following libraries will be used:

  • Pandas: Data loading and manipulation
  • NumPy: Numerical calculations
  • Matplotlib: Data visualization
  • Prophet: Time-series forecasting
  • Scikit-learn: Model evaluation
In [3]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from prophet import Prophet

from sklearn.metrics import (
    mean_absolute_error,
    mean_squared_error
)

📥 6. Dataset Loading¶

The Daily Minimum Temperature Dataset will be loaded from a public data repository.

After loading the dataset, we will display its first few records.

In [4]:
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jbrownlee/Datasets/master/daily-min-temperatures.csv"

df = pd.read_csv(url)

df.head()
Out[4]:
Date Temp
0 1981-01-01 20.7
1 1981-01-02 17.9
2 1981-01-03 18.8
3 1981-01-04 14.6
4 1981-01-05 15.8

🔍 7. Initial Dataset Inspection¶

Before preparing the forecasting model, we will inspect:

  • Dataset shape
  • Column names
  • Data types
  • Missing values
  • Duplicate records
  • Statistical summary
In [5]:
print("Dataset Shape:", df.shape)

print("\nColumn Names:")
print(df.columns.tolist())

print("\nData Types:")
print(df.dtypes)

print("\nMissing Values:")
print(df.isnull().sum())

print("\nDuplicate Rows:")
print(df.duplicated().sum())

print("\nStatistical Summary:")
display(df.describe())

🛠️ 8. Data Preprocessing¶

Prophet requires two specially named columns:

  • ds: Date or timestamp column
  • y: Numerical target column

Therefore, we will:

  1. Convert the Date column into datetime format
  2. Rename Date as ds
  3. Rename Temp as y
  4. Sort the observations chronologically
  5. Remove duplicate dates
  6. Handle missing temperature values
In [6]:
df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["Date"], errors="coerce")

df = df.rename(columns={
    "Date": "ds",
    "Temp": "y"
})

df = df.sort_values("ds")

df = df.drop_duplicates(subset="ds")

df.head()
Out[6]:
ds y
0 1981-01-01 20.7
1 1981-01-02 17.9
2 1981-01-03 18.8
3 1981-01-04 14.6
4 1981-01-05 15.8

🧹 9. Handling Missing Values¶

Missing dates or temperature values can affect forecasting accuracy.

We will:

  • Remove records with invalid dates
  • Convert the target column to numeric
  • Use interpolation to fill missing temperature observations
In [7]:
df["y"] = pd.to_numeric(df["y"], errors="coerce")

df = df.dropna(subset=["ds"])

df["y"] = df["y"].interpolate(
    method="linear",
    limit_direction="both"
)

print("Missing Values After Cleaning:")
print(df.isnull().sum())

print("\nCleaned Dataset Shape:", df.shape)

Missing Values After Cleaning:
ds    0
y     0
dtype: int64

Cleaned Dataset Shape: (3650, 2)

📅 10. Time-Series Range¶

We will now check:

  • Starting date
  • Ending date
  • Total observations
  • Minimum temperature
  • Maximum temperature
  • Average temperature
In [8]:
print("Starting Date:", df["ds"].min())
print("Ending Date:", df["ds"].max())
print("Total Observations:", len(df))

print("\nMinimum Temperature:", df["y"].min(), "°C")
print("Maximum Temperature:", df["y"].max(), "°C")
print("Average Temperature:", round(df["y"].mean(), 2), "°C")

Starting Date: 1981-01-01 00:00:00
Ending Date: 1990-12-31 00:00:00
Total Observations: 3650

Minimum Temperature: 0.0 °C
Maximum Temperature: 26.3 °C
Average Temperature: 11.18 °C

📈 11. Historical Temperature Visualization¶

The following graph shows how daily minimum temperature changes over time.

This visualization helps identify:

  • Seasonal fluctuations
  • Long-term temperature patterns
  • Unusually high or low observations
  • Yearly repeating behaviour
In [9]:
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 6))

plt.plot(
    df["ds"],
    df["y"],
    linewidth=1
)

plt.title(
    "Daily Minimum Temperature Over Time",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)", fontsize=12)
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

📊 12. Rolling Average Analysis¶

Daily temperature data may contain frequent short-term fluctuations.

A 30-day rolling average smooths the series and makes the overall temperature pattern easier to understand.

In [10]:
df["30_Day_Rolling_Mean"] = df["y"].rolling(window=30).mean()

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 6))

plt.plot(
    df["ds"],
    df["y"],
    label="Daily Temperature",
    alpha=0.5
)

plt.plot(
    df["ds"],
    df["30_Day_Rolling_Mean"],
    label="30-Day Rolling Average",
    linewidth=2
)

plt.title(
    "Daily Temperature and 30-Day Rolling Average",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

🗓️ 13. Monthly Temperature Analysis¶

To understand yearly seasonality, we will calculate the average minimum temperature for each month.

This helps identify warmer and cooler periods of the year.

In [11]:
monthly_average = (
    df.assign(Month=df["ds"].dt.month)
      .groupby("Month")["y"]
      .mean()
)

monthly_average
Out[11]:
Month
1     15.030323
2     15.373759
3     14.565484
4     12.088333
5      9.866452
6      7.278333
7      6.692581
8      7.891290
9      8.976333
10    10.309355
11    12.479667
12    13.851948
Name: y, dtype: float64
In [12]:
month_names = [
    "Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr",
    "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug",
    "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"
]

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

plt.bar(month_names, monthly_average.values)

plt.title(
    "Average Minimum Temperature by Month",
    fontsize=16,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("Average Minimum Temperature (°C)")
plt.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

✂️ 14. Train-Test Split¶

Time-series observations must remain in chronological order.

Random shuffling is not appropriate because future observations should not be used to predict past values.

We will use:

  • All observations except the final 365 days for training
  • The final 365 days for testing

The testing dataset therefore represents approximately one complete year.

In [13]:
test_days = 365

train = df[["ds", "y"]].iloc[:-test_days].copy()
test = df[["ds", "y"]].iloc[-test_days:].copy()

print("Training Observations:", len(train))
print("Testing Observations:", len(test))

print("\nTraining Period:")
print(train["ds"].min(), "to", train["ds"].max())

print("\nTesting Period:")
print(test["ds"].min(), "to", test["ds"].max())

Training Observations: 3285
Testing Observations: 365

Training Period:
1981-01-01 00:00:00 to 1989-12-31 00:00:00

Testing Period:
1990-01-01 00:00:00 to 1990-12-31 00:00:00

📉 15. Training and Testing Data Visualization¶

The following graph displays the chronological division of the dataset.

  • The training section is used to learn temperature patterns.
  • The testing section is used to evaluate forecasting performance.
In [14]:
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 6))

plt.plot(
    train["ds"],
    train["y"],
    label="Training Data"
)

plt.plot(
    test["ds"],
    test["y"],
    label="Testing Data"
)

plt.title(
    "Training and Testing Temperature Data",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

🤖 16. Prophet Model Building¶

Prophet models a time series using major components such as:

  • Trend: Long-term increase or decrease
  • Yearly seasonality: Patterns repeating every year
  • Weekly seasonality: Patterns repeating every week
  • Holiday effects: Changes caused by special events, when supplied

For daily temperature data:

  • Yearly seasonality is important.
  • Weekly seasonality is not expected to have a major physical meaning.
  • Daily seasonality is unnecessary because there is one observation per day.
In [15]:
prophet_model = Prophet(
    yearly_seasonality=True,
    weekly_seasonality=False,
    daily_seasonality=False,
    seasonality_mode="additive",
    interval_width=0.95
)

🏋️ 17. Model Training¶

The Prophet model will now be trained using the historical training dataset.

During training, the model learns:

  • Long-term temperature trends
  • Repeating yearly temperature patterns
  • Seasonal variation
  • Changes in the time-series growth pattern
In [16]:
prophet_model.fit(train)

00:04:54 - cmdstanpy - INFO - Chain [1] start processing
00:04:59 - cmdstanpy - INFO - Chain [1] done processing
Out[16]:
<prophet.forecaster.Prophet at 0x24bf3915e80>

📅 18. Preparing Dates for Prediction¶

Prophet requires a DataFrame containing a ds column for the dates that need to be forecasted.

We will provide the dates available in the testing dataset.

In [17]:
test_dates = test[["ds"]].copy()

test_dates.head()
Out[17]:
ds
3285 1990-01-01
3286 1990-01-02
3287 1990-01-03
3288 1990-01-04
3289 1990-01-05

🔮 19. Temperature Forecasting¶

The trained Prophet model will now forecast minimum temperatures for every date in the testing period.

Prophet returns several columns, including:

  • yhat: Forecasted temperature
  • yhat_lower: Lower forecast limit
  • yhat_upper: Upper forecast limit
  • Trend and seasonal components
In [18]:
forecast = prophet_model.predict(test_dates)

forecast[
    ["ds", "yhat", "yhat_lower", "yhat_upper"]
].head()
Out[18]:
ds yhat yhat_lower yhat_upper
0 1990-01-01 14.851055 9.711926 20.290288
1 1990-01-02 14.914131 9.357358 20.227992
2 1990-01-03 14.974986 9.737992 20.061964
3 1990-01-04 15.032813 9.741954 20.257468
4 1990-01-05 15.086868 9.652461 20.258350

📏 20. Model Evaluation¶

The Prophet model will be evaluated using three metrics.

Mean Absolute Error¶

MAE measures the average absolute difference between actual and predicted temperatures.

Root Mean Squared Error¶

RMSE gives greater importance to large forecasting errors.

Mean Absolute Percentage Error¶

MAPE expresses the average forecasting error as a percentage.

Lower MAE, RMSE and MAPE values indicate better model performance.

In [19]:
actual = test["y"].to_numpy()
predicted = forecast["yhat"].to_numpy()

mae = mean_absolute_error(actual, predicted)

rmse = np.sqrt(
    mean_squared_error(actual, predicted)
)

non_zero = actual != 0

mape = np.mean(
    np.abs(
        (actual[non_zero] - predicted[non_zero])
        / actual[non_zero]
    )
) * 100

print("Prophet Model Evaluation")
print("-" * 35)
print(f"MAE  : {mae:.2f} °C")
print(f"RMSE : {rmse:.2f} °C")
print(f"MAPE : {mape:.2f}%")

Prophet Model Evaluation
-----------------------------------
MAE  : 1.93 °C
RMSE : 2.51 °C
MAPE : 19.74%

📉 21. Forecast Visualization¶

The following visualization compares:

  • Historical training temperatures
  • Actual testing temperatures
  • Prophet forecasted temperatures
  • 95% uncertainty interval

Forecast values closer to actual values indicate better model performance.

In [20]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 7))

plt.plot(
    train["ds"],
    train["y"],
    label="Training Data",
    linewidth=1
)

plt.plot(
    test["ds"],
    test["y"],
    label="Actual Temperature",
    linewidth=1.5
)

plt.plot(
    forecast["ds"],
    forecast["yhat"],
    label="Prophet Forecast",
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2
)

plt.fill_between(
    forecast["ds"],
    forecast["yhat_lower"],
    forecast["yhat_upper"],
    alpha=0.2,
    label="95% Uncertainty Interval"
)

plt.title(
    "Prophet Forecast vs Actual Temperature",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date", fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)", fontsize=12)
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The following graph displays only the testing period.

This provides a clearer comparison between actual and forecasted daily temperatures.

In [21]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 7))

plt.plot(
    test["ds"],
    test["y"],
    label="Actual Temperature",
    linewidth=1.5
)

plt.plot(
    forecast["ds"],
    forecast["yhat"],
    label="Forecasted Temperature",
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2
)

plt.fill_between(
    forecast["ds"],
    forecast["yhat_lower"],
    forecast["yhat_upper"],
    alpha=0.2,
    label="95% Uncertainty Interval"
)

plt.title(
    "Testing Period: Actual vs Forecasted Temperature",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

📋 23. Actual and Forecasted Values¶

The following table provides a date-wise comparison of:

  • Actual temperature
  • Forecasted temperature
  • Lower forecast limit
  • Upper forecast limit
  • Absolute forecasting error
In [22]:
results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Date": test["ds"].to_numpy(),
    "Actual Temperature": actual,
    "Forecasted Temperature": predicted,
    "Lower Limit": forecast["yhat_lower"].to_numpy(),
    "Upper Limit": forecast["yhat_upper"].to_numpy()
})

results["Absolute Error"] = np.abs(
    results["Actual Temperature"]
    - results["Forecasted Temperature"]
)

results.head(15).round(2)

🧩 24. Prophet Forecast Components¶

Prophet separates the forecast into interpretable components.

For this temperature dataset, the important components include:

  • Trend: Long-term movement in temperature
  • Yearly seasonality: Repeating annual temperature cycle

The component plots help us understand how each pattern contributes to the final forecast.

In [23]:
prophet_model.plot_components(forecast)

plt.suptitle(
    "Prophet Forecast Components",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold",
    y=1.02
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

💡 25. Result Interpretation¶

The Prophet model learns the long-term and seasonal patterns present in daily temperature data.

The results can be interpreted as follows:

  • A lower MAE indicates a smaller average temperature prediction error.
  • A lower RMSE indicates fewer large forecasting errors.
  • A lower MAPE indicates better percentage-based accuracy.
  • Forecasts close to actual observations indicate good model performance.
  • The uncertainty interval represents the possible range of future temperature values.
  • Yearly seasonality shows how temperature patterns repeat across different months.

Daily weather may also be affected by unexpected atmospheric conditions, so exact forecasts are difficult and some error is natural.

🚀 26. Training the Final Prophet Model¶

After evaluating the model, we will train a new Prophet model using the complete dataset.

This allows the final model to learn from all available temperature observations before forecasting future dates.

In [24]:
final_data = df[["ds", "y"]].copy()

final_model = Prophet(
    yearly_seasonality=True,
    weekly_seasonality=False,
    daily_seasonality=False,
    seasonality_mode="additive",
    interval_width=0.95
)

final_model.fit(final_data)

00:07:34 - cmdstanpy - INFO - Chain [1] start processing
00:07:34 - cmdstanpy - INFO - Chain [1] done processing
Out[24]:
<prophet.forecaster.Prophet at 0x24bf4735d10>

📆 27. Creating Future Dates¶

We will create daily timestamps for the next 365 days.

The future DataFrame contains:

  • Historical dates
  • 365 new future dates
In [25]:
future_dates = final_model.make_future_dataframe(
    periods=365,
    freq="D"
)

future_dates.tail()
Out[25]:
ds
4010 1991-12-27
4011 1991-12-28
4012 1991-12-29
4013 1991-12-30
4014 1991-12-31

🔭 28. Forecasting the Next 365 Days¶

The final Prophet model will now forecast daily minimum temperatures for the next year.

The output contains:

  • Predicted temperature
  • Lower uncertainty limit
  • Upper uncertainty limit
  • Trend and seasonal components
In [26]:
future_forecast = final_model.predict(future_dates)

future_forecast[
    ["ds", "yhat", "yhat_lower", "yhat_upper"]
].tail()

ds yhat yhat_lower yhat_upper

📑 29. Future Temperature Forecast Table¶

The following table displays the predicted minimum temperatures for the next 365 days.

Only future dates are selected from the complete forecast.

In [27]:
last_historical_date = final_data["ds"].max()

future_only = future_forecast[
    future_forecast["ds"] > last_historical_date
][
    ["ds", "yhat", "yhat_lower", "yhat_upper"]
].copy()

future_only.columns = [
    "Date",
    "Forecasted Temperature",
    "Lower Limit",
    "Upper Limit"
]

future_only.head(15).round(2)

📊 30. Future Temperature Forecast Visualization¶

The final graph displays:

  • Historical minimum temperatures
  • Forecasted temperatures for the next 365 days
  • Forecast uncertainty interval

This visualization shows the expected yearly temperature cycle.

In [28]:
future_only_plot = future_forecast[
    future_forecast["ds"] > last_historical_date
].copy()

plt.figure(figsize=(16, 7))

plt.plot(
    final_data["ds"],
    final_data["y"],
    label="Historical Temperature",
    linewidth=1
)

plt.plot(
    future_only_plot["ds"],
    future_only_plot["yhat"],
    label="Next 365-Day Forecast",
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2
)

plt.fill_between(
    future_only_plot["ds"],
    future_only_plot["yhat_lower"],
    future_only_plot["yhat_upper"],
    alpha=0.2,
    label="95% Uncertainty Interval"
)

plt.title(
    "Future Daily Minimum Temperature Forecast using Prophet",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

🌍 31. Complete Prophet Forecast¶

Prophet provides a built-in forecast visualization containing:

  • Historical observations
  • Forecasted trend
  • Future predictions
  • Forecast uncertainty

This provides an overall view of the complete time-series forecast.

In [29]:
final_model.plot(future_forecast)

plt.title(
    "Complete Prophet Temperature Forecast",
    fontsize=17,
    fontweight="bold"
)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Minimum Temperature (°C)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

✅ 32. Conclusion¶

In this notebook, we successfully implemented the Prophet time-series forecasting algorithm to predict daily minimum temperatures.

Tasks completed¶

  • Loaded the Daily Minimum Temperature Dataset
  • Inspected and cleaned the dataset
  • Converted columns into Prophet format
  • Visualized historical temperature observations
  • Analysed rolling and monthly temperature patterns
  • Created a chronological train-test split
  • Built and trained the Prophet model
  • Forecasted testing-period temperatures
  • Evaluated the model using MAE, RMSE and MAPE
  • Visualized actual and forecasted temperatures
  • Analysed Prophet trend and yearly seasonal components
  • Trained a final model on the complete dataset
  • Forecasted minimum temperatures for the next 365 days

Prophet is a flexible and interpretable forecasting algorithm that works effectively with time-series data containing trend and seasonal patterns.

In [ ]:
